worth_it_explanation: Pacing decides how a guaranteed campaign spends its impressions across its flight, and under-delivery decides whether a publisher hits the numbers it sold or owes a make-good. A line item that front-loads burns through its inventory early and starves later demand, while one that falls behind quietly misses its guarantee until the flight is almost over. This check reads the pacing settings and the delivery trend a publisher already has in FreeWheel, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete line item the publisher can re-pace or escalate before the miss becomes a credit.
intro: FreeWheel is a video and Connected TV (CTV) ad server that broadcasters and large publishers use to deliver guaranteed, direct-sold campaigns against their video inventory. Pacing controls how a line item spreads its committed impressions across the days of its flight, and under-delivery is what happens when the line item cannot reach the goal it was sold against. This subcategory is about the delivery health of guaranteed line items, whether they pace smoothly toward their goals and whether the publisher catches a shortfall early enough to act. These checks confirm that pacing is set deliberately for each line item and that under-delivery risk is monitored rather than discovered at the end of the flight.

audit_checks.title: Line items front-load or under-pace instead of spreading evenly across the flight
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A guaranteed line item that front-loads spends its impressions early, then starves later dates of inventory and crowds out other campaigns that were counting on the same avails. A line item that under-paces drifts behind its goal and leaves the shortfall to surface near the end of the flight when there is little runway to recover. Either pattern threatens the guarantee the publisher sold and the delivery of the campaigns sharing the same inventory.

educate.base:
A guaranteed line item in FreeWheel is sold against a goal, for example a number of impressions or completed views to deliver across a flight that runs from a start date to an end date. Pacing is the rule that decides how the line item spreads that goal across the days in between. Even pacing aims to deliver a steady share each day so the line item arrives at its goal on the last day of the flight. As-fast-as-possible pacing instead delivers as much as the inventory allows whenever the targeting matches, with no daily smoothing. The pacing setting is a deliberate choice, and the wrong choice for a given line item shows up as a delivery curve that does not match the flight.

Front-loading is the failure mode of pacing that is too aggressive for the inventory. The line item spends a large part of its goal in the first days, then runs short of avails for the rest of the flight because it already consumed the impressions it needed later. In a video and Connected TV (CTV) ad server, where ad pods hold a fixed number of slots and premium avails are finite, a front-loaded line item also crowds out the other guaranteed campaigns competing for the same pods, so the damage is not contained to one line item.

Under-pacing is the opposite. The line item delivers below the rate it needs to reach its goal, so a gap opens between where delivery is and where the flight says it should be. The gap can come from a goal set too high for the available inventory, targeting that is narrower than the demand assumed, or competing higher-priority line items absorbing the avails. A small daily gap looks harmless until it compounds across the flight into a shortfall too large to close in the days that remain.

A few signs point to a line item whose pacing does not fit its flight:
  • The delivery curve is steep early and flat later, which is the shape of front-loading
  • Delivered impressions sit well below the straight-line target for the day in the flight, which is the shape of under-pacing
  • The pacing setting is as-fast-as-possible on a line item whose flight is long enough that even pacing was the intent
  • A high-priority line item consistently leaves lower-priority guaranteed line items short of their daily share
You may want to confirm that each guaranteed line item carries the pacing setting its flight and inventory call for, and that the delivery curve tracks the flight rather than spiking early or drifting behind. One approach is to read the delivery trend for each line item against its straight-line target and re-pace the ones whose curve does not fit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, premium video avails such as the opening pod of a popular show or a live event are finite and in demand. A front-loaded line item can drain those premium pods early and leave later airings short, so you may want to hold even pacing on the line items aimed at high-demand programming and watch that no single line item consumes a pod's slots ahead of the campaigns sharing it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property running shoppable or product video, a campaign tied to a sale window or a product launch carries a flight that maps to a real calendar event. You may want to confirm the pacing matches that calendar, so a line item meant to run evenly through a launch week does not front-load into the first day and go dark before the sale ends.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for line item pacing and delivery settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the pacing setting on each guaranteed line item and read its delivery curve against the straight-line target for its point in the flight, looking for early spikes or a widening shortfall.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel UI
steps: Open the campaign or media plan that holds the guaranteed line items and list the active line items with their flight start and end dates.
For each line item, note the pacing setting, for example even pacing or as-fast-as-possible, and the goal it was sold against.
Open the delivery view for each line item and compare delivered impressions to the straight-line target for the current day in the flight.
Record any line item whose delivery is steep early then flat, or whose delivered total sits well below the straight-line target, and note the pacing setting on each.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a delivery report by line item broken out by day, with delivered impressions or completed views and the daily goal.
Plot or read the daily delivery against the expected daily share to see whether the curve spikes early or drifts behind the flight.
Note the line items whose delivery curve does not track their flight and the size of any gap between delivered and target.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per guaranteed line item, the pacing setting, the flight dates, and the delivery curve against the straight-line target. A line item whose delivery tracks the flight, with the pacing setting matching the inventory and the goal, passes. A line item that front-loads its goal early or sits well below its straight-line target, especially where the pacing setting does not fit the flight, is a fail recorded with the line item, its pacing setting, and the shape of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pacing setting and the delivery curve against the straight-line target for each line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that guaranteed line items on {{site_name}} pace in a way that does not fit their flights, with delivery spiking early on some and drifting behind the straight-line target on others. A front-loaded line item drains finite video and Connected TV (CTV) avails early and crowds out the campaigns sharing the same pods, while an under-pacing line item lets a daily gap compound into a shortfall too large to close late in the flight. {{voice_recommend}} reading each line item's delivery curve against its straight-line target, then re-pacing the ones whose curve does not track the flight so they spread their goal evenly rather than spending early or falling behind. After the change, recheck the delivery curves to confirm each line item is back on pace toward its goal.

audit_checks.title: Under-delivery risk is not monitored, so make-goods and missed guarantees surface late
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A guaranteed campaign that falls behind its goal becomes a missed guarantee, which the publisher owes back as a make-good or a credit. Without a regular read on delivery against goal across the flight, the shortfall surfaces near the end when there is no runway to add inventory or shift priority, so a problem that was recoverable early becomes a revenue loss the publisher cannot avoid.

educate.base:
A guaranteed campaign carries an obligation. The publisher sold a number of impressions or completed views, and if the campaign delivers less than that by the end of its flight, the publisher has to make the buyer whole, usually by running a make-good campaign or issuing a credit. Under-delivery monitoring is the practice of reading, while the flight is still running, how each guaranteed line item is tracking against the goal it has to reach, so a shortfall can be caught while there is still time to act on it.

The cost of catching a shortfall late is that the remedies shrink as the flight runs out. Early in a flight, a line item that is tracking behind can be helped by widening its targeting, raising its priority so it wins more avails, or adding inventory it can run against. Late in the flight, those same remedies have little time to work because the days that remain cannot hold the impressions the line item already missed. A shortfall found on the last day of the flight is no longer a problem to solve, it is a make-good to schedule.

FreeWheel reports delivery against goal for each line item, so the data needed to see a developing shortfall exists. The audit question is whether anyone reads it on a regular cadence. A delivery report that is pulled only at the end of the flight, or a pacing alert that exists but is not configured or watched, leaves the publisher reacting to misses rather than preventing them.

A few signs point to under-delivery risk that is not being monitored:
  • No regular cadence exists for reviewing delivery against goal while flights are live, for example a weekly or daily pacing read
  • Pacing or under-delivery alerts are available in FreeWheel but are not configured, or fire to no one who acts on them
  • Make-goods and missed guarantees are discovered at the end of the flight rather than flagged mid-flight
  • The publisher cannot name which live line items are currently at risk of missing their goal
You may want to confirm that delivery against goal is read on a regular cadence while flights are live, and that an under-delivery signal reaches someone who can act on it. One approach is to set a recurring pacing review and configure FreeWheel's delivery alerts so a line item tracking behind is flagged while there is still runway to recover.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, guaranteed campaigns tied to tentpole programming or a live event have a fixed window, and a shortfall on a one-time event cannot be made good in the same context. You may want to monitor delivery against goal more closely on event-tied line items since a miss there forces a make-good in different inventory that may not carry the same value to the buyer.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property, a guaranteed video campaign tied to a promotion or a product launch has to deliver inside the promotion window to matter to the buyer. You may want to read delivery against goal frequently during the window, so a line item tracking behind can be helped while the promotion is still live rather than after it closes.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for delivery reporting and pacing alerts is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether delivery against goal is read on a regular cadence while flights are live and whether an under-delivery signal reaches someone who can act, rather than surfacing only at the end of the flight.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel UI
steps: Open the active media plans and list the guaranteed line items that are currently in flight with their goals and end dates.
For each line item, read delivered to date against the goal and the days remaining in the flight to gauge whether it is on track.
Check whether pacing or under-delivery alerts are configured and who they notify.
Ask the publisher how often delivery against goal is reviewed while flights are live, and record the cadence and who owns it.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a delivery report across active line items with delivered to date, goal, percent to goal, and days remaining in the flight.
Flag any line item whose percent to goal is well below its percent of the flight elapsed, which marks a developing shortfall.
Note whether such a report is pulled on a recurring cadence or only at the end of the flight, and record the line items currently at risk.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a recurring review of delivery against goal exists for live flights, whether under-delivery alerts are configured and reach someone who acts, and which live line items are currently tracking behind. A setup with a regular pacing cadence and alerts that surface at-risk line items mid-flight passes. A setup where delivery is read only at the end of the flight, or where alerts are unconfigured or unwatched, is a fail recorded with the line items at risk and the gap in the monitoring.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether delivery against goal is reviewed on a regular cadence and which live line items are tracking behind above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor under-delivery risk on a regular cadence while guaranteed flights are live, so shortfalls surface near the end of the flight rather than mid-flight. A guaranteed campaign that misses its goal becomes a make-good or a credit, and the remedies that could close a gap, such as widening targeting or raising priority, lose their effect once the flight is nearly over. {{voice_recommend}} setting a recurring review of delivery against goal across live line items and configuring FreeWheel's pacing alerts so a line item tracking behind reaches someone who can act while there is still runway. After the change, confirm that at-risk line items are flagged mid-flight rather than discovered as misses at the end.
